Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA, Judith Prakash JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JAD, Chao Hick Tin SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd (in liquidation)

Facts.Text: Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd was a company in Singapore.
Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd was placed into liquidation before the filing of Civil Appeal No 202 of 2020 and was described as being in liquidation in the appeal title.
At the material time in 2020, Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd and Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd were related companies referred to collectively as the Companies.
At the material time in 2020, Lim Chee Meng and Lim Huey Ching were directors of Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, after Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd was placed under interim judicial management and then judicial management, its directors could, without approval from the interim judicial managers, judicial managers or liquidators, cause the company to commence and maintain applications in its name to restrain the company’s existing law firm from acting for the insolvency officeholders and the company.
Party_Details.Rule: Under the Singapore Companies Act and the judicial management regime, when a company is placed under interim judicial management or judicial management, the court order and section 227G provide that the affairs, business and property of the company are to be managed by the interim judicial managers or judicial managers, who exercise all powers and entitlements of directors, including the power to bring or defend legal proceedings in the name and on behalf of the company. Directors may retain only narrow residual powers at common law in situations where the company seeks to challenge or appeal against the very order appointing insolvency officeholders or placing the company into an insolvency process.
Party_Details.Application: Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd was placed under interim judicial management on 2020-04-27 and under judicial management on 2020-08-07. The interim judicial management and judicial management orders provided that the interim judicial managers and later judicial managers would manage the affairs, business and property of the company and could exercise all powers and entitlements of directors, including commencing or defending legal proceedings and retaining legal counsel. After these appointments, the interim judicial managers and judicial managers retained Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP to act for the company. In July 2020, while interim judicial managers were in place, the directors Lim Chee Meng and Lim Huey Ching caused injunctive applications to be filed in the company’s name to restrain Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP from acting for the company and the insolvency officeholders, without seeking or obtaining approval from the interim judicial managers. After the judicial management order on 2020-08-07, they did not seek consent from the judicial managers to proceed with those applications. The injunctive applications did not seek to challenge or appeal against the interim judicial management order, the judicial management order, or the later winding up order. Instead, they sought to affect the choice of legal representation for the company and its insolvency officeholders, a matter that fell within the management powers conferred on the interim judicial managers, judicial managers and later liquidators. In this context, the directors’ actions in causing the company to commence and maintain the injunctive applications were outside the narrow category of residual powers recognised at common law and overlapped with powers that the insolvency officeholders were authorised to exercise.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal analysed the statutory framework of judicial management and liquidation under the Companies Act, focusing on section 227G and the Eleventh Schedule, and the terms of the interim judicial management and judicial management orders made in respect of Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd. The court held that upon the making of these orders, all management powers conferred on directors, including the power to commence or defend proceedings in the company’s name and to retain or discharge solicitors, were transferred to the interim judicial managers and judicial managers. The court reviewed English and Malaysian authorities, including Re Union Accident Insurance Co Ltd, Sri Hartamas Development Sdn Bhd v MBF Finance Bhd, Taman Sungai Dua Development Sdn Bhd v Goh Boon Kim, and other cases, and accepted that directors may retain only narrow residual powers at common law, such as instructing solicitors to oppose a winding up petition or to appeal against or otherwise challenge the very order appointing insolvency officeholders or placing the company into an insolvency process. The court rejected the directors’ argument that they had a broader residual power to commence proceedings whenever they considered it inappropriate for insolvency officeholders to act, finding that such a view would undermine the statutory scheme and allow directors to interfere with the management of the company after judicial management or liquidation. The court distinguished authorities such as Shanks v Central Regional Council and In re Lehman Bros Europe Ltd (in administration) (No 9), noting differences in statutory context and the presence in the UK regime of provisions allowing directors to exercise certain management powers with administrators’ consent. Applying these principles, the court concluded that the injunctive applications filed in the name of Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd did not fall within the narrow residual powers because they did not challenge the interim judicial management order, the judicial management order, or the winding up order, but instead sought to restrain the law firm chosen by the insolvency officeholders. The court held that the directors lacked authority to cause the company to commence and maintain those applications, and that the applications were properly struck out for want of authority. On costs, the court applied the principles in DB Trustees (Hong Kong) Ltd v Consult Asia Pte Ltd, finding that the Lim directors were the real actors behind the litigation, that their conduct caused Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP to incur costs, and that it was just for them, as non-parties to the appeals, to bear the costs personally rather than the Companies’ creditors. The appeals were dismissed and costs of $40,000 inclusive of disbursements were ordered against Lim Chee Meng and Lim Huey Ching personally.

Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA, Judith Prakash JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JAD, Chao Hick Tin SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd (under Interim Judicial Management)

Facts.Text: At the time of Civil Appeal No 203 of 2020, Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd was described as being under interim judicial management in the appeal title.
At the material time in 2020, Lim Chee Meng and Lim Huey Ching were directors of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd.
On 2020-05-12, the High Court made an order placing Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd under interim judicial management and appointing interim judicial managers over the company.
With effect from 2020-05-12, the affairs, business and property of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd were to be managed by the interim judicial managers under the interim judicial management order.

The interim judicial management order for Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd authorised the interim judicial managers to exercise all powers and entitlements of a judicial manager and all powers and entitlements of directors of the company conferred by the Companies Act and the company’s memorandum and articles of association.
After their appointment on 2020-05-12, the interim judicial managers of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd retained the legal services of Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP to act for the company.
From 2020-05-12, Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP acted as legal counsel for the interim judicial managers of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd pursuant to a retainer.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, after Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd was placed under interim judicial management and then judicial management, its directors could, without approval from the interim judicial managers, judicial managers or liquidators, cause the company to commence and maintain applications in its name to restrain the company’s existing law firm from acting for the insolvency officeholders and the company.
Party_Details.Rule: Under the Singapore Companies Act and the judicial management regime, when a company is placed under interim judicial management or judicial management, the court order and section 227G provide that the affairs, business and property of the company are to be managed by the interim judicial managers or judicial managers, who exercise all powers and entitlements of directors, including the power to bring or defend legal proceedings in the name and on behalf of the company and to retain or discharge legal counsel. Directors may retain only narrow residual powers at common law in situations where the company seeks to challenge or appeal against the very order appointing insolvency officeholders or placing the company into an insolvency process.
Party_Details.Application: Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd was placed under interim judicial management on 2020-05-12 and under judicial management on 2020-08-07. The interim judicial management and judicial management orders provided that the interim judicial managers and later judicial managers would manage the affairs, business and property of the company and could exercise all powers and entitlements of directors, including commencing or defending legal proceedings and retaining legal counsel. After these appointments, the interim judicial managers and judicial managers retained Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP to act for the company. In July 2020, while interim judicial managers were in place, the directors Lim Chee Meng and Lim Huey Ching caused injunctive applications to be filed in the company’s name to restrain Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP from acting for the company and the insolvency officeholders, without seeking or obtaining approval from the interim judicial managers. After the judicial management order on 2020-08-07, they did not seek consent from the judicial managers to proceed with those applications. The injunctive applications did not seek to challenge or appeal against the interim judicial management order, the judicial management order, or the later winding up order. Instead, they sought to affect the choice of legal representation for the company and its insolvency officeholders, a matter that fell within the management powers conferred on the interim judicial managers, judicial managers and later liquidators. In this context, the directors’ actions in causing the company to commence and maintain the injunctive applications were outside the narrow category of residual powers recognised at common law and overlapped with powers that the insolvency officeholders were authorised to exercise.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal applied the same statutory and common law analysis to Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd as to Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd. It held that the interim judicial management and judicial management orders, read with section 227G and the Eleventh Schedule of the Companies Act, transferred all management powers of the directors, including the power to commence or defend proceedings and to retain or discharge solicitors, to the interim judicial managers and judicial managers. The court accepted that directors may retain only narrow residual powers at common law, such as instructing solicitors to oppose a winding up petition or to appeal against or otherwise challenge the very order appointing insolvency officeholders or placing the company into an insolvency process. The court rejected the directors’ contention that they had a broader residual power to commence proceedings whenever they considered it inappropriate for insolvency officeholders to act, finding that such a position would undermine the statutory scheme and allow directors to interfere with the management of the company after judicial management or liquidation. The court distinguished authorities from receivership and administration contexts and emphasised that the injunctive applications filed in the name of Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd did not challenge the interim judicial management order, the judicial management order, or the subsequent winding up order, but instead sought to restrain the law firm chosen by the insolvency officeholders. The court concluded that the directors lacked authority to cause the company to commence and maintain those applications, and that the applications were properly struck out for want of authority. On costs, the court held that the Lim directors were the real actors behind the litigation, that their conduct caused Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP to incur costs, and that it was just for them, as non-parties to the appeals, to bear the costs personally rather than the Companies’ creditors. The appeals, including Civil Appeal No 203 of 2020 relating to Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd, were dismissed and costs of $40,000 inclusive of disbursements were ordered against Lim Chee Meng and Lim Huey Ching personally.

Metadata.Coram: Sundaresh Menon CJ, Andrew Phang Boon Leong JCA, Judith Prakash JCA, Belinda Ang Saw Ean JAD, Chao Hick Tin SJ
Metadata.Tribunal_Court: Court of Appeal of the Republic of Singapore

Party_Details.Name: Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP

Facts.Text: Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP is a Singapore law firm.
On 2020-04-27, interim judicial managers were appointed over Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd.
On 2020-05-12, interim judicial managers were appointed over Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd.
On 2020-04-27, Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd was placed under interim judicial management and interim judicial managers were appointed over it.
On 2020-05-12, Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd was placed under interim judicial management and interim judicial managers were appointed over it.
On 2020-08-07, Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd and Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd were placed under judicial management.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP could continue to act as legal counsel for the interim judicial managers, judicial managers and liquidators of Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd and Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd, and for the Companies themselves, in circumstances where the Companies’ directors had caused applications to be filed in the Companies’ names seeking to restrain the firm from acting.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a company is under interim judicial management, judicial management or liquidation, the insolvency officeholders appointed by the court manage the affairs, business and property of the company and exercise the powers and entitlements of directors, including the power to retain and instruct legal counsel and to commence or defend proceedings in the company’s name. Applications brought in the company’s name without authority from the insolvency officeholders may be struck out for lack of standing or authority.
Party_Details.Application: Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP was retained by the interim judicial managers of Hin Leong Trading (Pte) Ltd and Ocean Tankers (Pte) Ltd shortly after the interim judicial management orders on 2020-04-27 and 2020-05-12, and continued to act for the Companies after the judicial management orders on 2020-08-07. The interim judicial management and judicial management orders authorised the interim judicial managers and judicial managers to exercise all powers and entitlements of directors, including retaining legal counsel and conducting litigation in the Companies’ names. In July 2020, the Companies’ directors caused injunctive applications to be filed in the Companies’ names seeking to restrain Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP from acting for the Companies and the insolvency officeholders, without obtaining approval from the interim judicial managers or judicial managers. Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP responded by filing striking out applications on 2020-10-05, contending that the directors lacked authority to cause the Companies to commence and maintain the injunctive applications. The High Court allowed the striking out applications, and the Companies, through notices of appeal filed on 2020-11-27 and 2020-11-30, appealed that decision. The Court of Appeal considered whether the directors had authority to commence the injunctive applications and concluded that, after the Companies were placed under interim judicial management and judicial management, only the insolvency officeholders could decide whether to retain or discharge Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP and whether to commence proceedings in the Companies’ names. The injunctive applications, having been commenced without such authority, were struck out, and Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP remained entitled to act for the insolvency officeholders and the Companies.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal treated Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP as the respondent law firm whose standing to act was challenged by applications filed in the Companies’ names. The court did not make findings of liability against the firm. Instead, it focused on whether the Companies’ directors had authority to commence the injunctive applications. The court held that, under the judicial management and liquidation framework, the power to retain and instruct solicitors and to commence or defend proceedings in the company’s name is vested in the interim judicial managers, judicial managers and liquidators, not in the directors. The court accepted that the directors may retain only narrow residual powers at common law, such as instructing solicitors to oppose a winding up petition or to appeal against or otherwise challenge the very order appointing insolvency officeholders or placing the company into an insolvency process. The injunctive applications did not fall within this narrow category because they did not challenge the interim judicial management orders, the judicial management orders, or the winding up orders, but instead sought to restrain the law firm chosen by the insolvency officeholders. The court concluded that the directors lacked authority to cause the Companies to commence and maintain the injunctive applications, and that the applications were properly struck out. As a result, Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP was entitled to its costs of the appeals, which the court ordered to be borne personally by the directors Lim Chee Meng and Lim Huey Ching. The court did not find that Rajah & Tann Singapore LLP had engaged in any wrongful conduct in acting for the insolvency officeholders and the Companies.
